Field Trip Report · Perception Sketching

Rishikesh
Two Days of Observation

Sketching by the Ganges, navigating crowds, and real public transport

DurationTwo Days
StructureSelf-Directed
FocusNature & Architecture
LocationRishikesh, Uttarakhand

01

Overview

Rishikesh is a town built around the Ganges river in Uttarakhand. We spent two days there sketching at different spots, from the ghats (riverside steps) to the high suspension bridges that cross the river. The visit was self-directed with no strict schedule — we had the freedom to walk around, sketch, and observe on our own pace.

Unlike FRI which felt structured and calm, Rishikesh was the opposite: crowded, loud, and constantly moving. It was a different kind of challenge for perception and observation.

02

Places we visited

Most of our sketching happened near the river. The two main bridges (Ram Jhula and Laxman Jhula) were key observation points, and we also spent time sketching the ghats and the architecture along the riverbank.

📍 Ram Jhula Bridge 📍 Laxman Jhula Bridge 📍 Riverside Ghats

03

Sketching by the water

The main focus was on capturing the architecture of the ghats and the suspension bridges. The ghats have a lot of interesting stone work and steps leading down to the water. The bridges are high and dramatic, creating strong perspective lines when you look down from them.

Sketching by the Ganges was initially awkward with so many people around watching. But then something shifted — people started complimenting the sketches, gathering around to see them. What began as uncomfortable became genuinely enjoyable.

04

Experience

Rishikesh is overwhelming in the best way. The energy, the crowds, the noise — it forces you to work fast and adapt. You can't sit in one spot for hours sketching perfection like you could at FRI. You have to find a good spot, work quickly, and be okay with what you get.

The public bus ride was part of the experience too. No seats, standing the whole route gripping the overhead handle while packed with other commuters. It's the kind of authentic, real experience that you can only get by actually using local transport instead of taking a cab.

05

Challenges

The heat was intense — sun beating down directly, sketching made you sweat, and there's limited shade by the water. Long walks from one bridge to another left everyone tired. And as the day went on, our phones started dying, which made coordination harder.

The group also scattered at different times. People wanted to go to different places, some wanted breaks, and managing where everyone was became tricky. There were small money-management issues too — splitting costs for transport and food.

🔋

Key Takeaway

Battery management and group coordination were harder than the actual sketching. These real logistical challenges became part of the learning experience — and actually inspired a hackathon project called "Safar app" to help with group trip management.

06

Conclusion

The Rishikesh trip was about observation under pressure. Crowds, heat, movement, and uncertainty — these forced us to make quick decisions and trust our sketching instincts. It was uncomfortable at times but also liberating because perfection wasn't the goal; capturing the essence of the place was.

The contrast with FRI was instructive. Rishikesh taught speed and adaptation. FRI taught patience and detail. Both are essential for perception sketching.
